_comment: WC2026 Round of 32, 2026-07-01, Seattle Stadium. KNOCKOUT: no draw -- level after 90' -> extra time -> penalties, so the deliverable is who ADVANCES. Routes: Belgium won Group G (1-1 Egypt, 0-0 Iran, 5-1 New Zealand; 5 pts, GF 6 GA 2, rating 86) -- quality but BLUNT vs organized sides (frustrated by Egypt/Iran) and only exploded vs an open New Zealand. Senegal qualified as a BEST THIRD from Group I (1-3 France, 1-3 Norway, 5-0 Iraq; 3 pts, GF 7 GA 6, GD +1, rating 82) -- attacking and physical (7 goals, 5 past Iraq) but LEAKY (conceded 3 each to France and Norway). This is one of the round's closest ties: a small class gap (86 vs 82), and BOTH sides are open/attacking rather than organized blocks -> an end-to-end, higher-scoring, competitive game (BTTS/Over live), NOT a containment grind. Belgium the SLIGHT favourite by class; Senegal a genuine, dangerous underdog that can win it. Near a coin-flip; high ET/penalty risk given how even it is. Full motivation, no rotation. Home = Belgium (nominal; neutral venue). Cards not modelled (referee TBD).

meta.stakes: Single-elimination: level after 90' -> extra time -> penalties. One of the round's closest ties: Belgium (quality but sometimes blunt) a slight favourite vs an attacking, physical, leaky Senegal. Both open -> an end-to-end, higher-scoring, competitive game, very live for extra time/penalties.

home.name: Belgium
notes: Quality (rating 86); can be blunt vs organized sides (1-1 Egypt, 0-0 Iran) but explodes vs open ones (5-1 NZ). vs an open Senegal -> should get chances. attack 1.25.
Slight favourite; open game vs an attacking side, so goals live.

away.name: Senegal
notes: Attacking and physical (7 goals, 5-0 Iraq); a real threat, not a low block. attack 1.05.
Leaky (conceded 3 each to France/Norway). defense 1.05 -> Belgium should score, but so can Senegal.
Dangerous underdog; end-to-end game, very live for ET/penalties.

_recal: First pass leaned 3 pts above the market on Senegal (29.3% vs ~27%): Senegal is dangerous but Belgium (86 vs 82) is the market's clearer favourite. Lifted BEL 1.50 -> 1.56 and trimmed SEN 1.18 -> 1.12 to align; still a near coin-flip, open game.

_postmatch.result: Belgium 3-2 Senegal after extra time (winner at 120+5). Senegal led 2-0 before Belgium clawed it back to 2-2 by 90', then edged it with the last kick of extra time -> Belgium advance. A five-goal thriller and a two-goal comeback.
_postmatch.model_1x2: Belgium 46.9 / Draw 26.4 / Senegal 26.7 (MEDIUM, 90'); advancement Belgium 61.4 / Senegal 38.6
_postmatch.verdict: Textbook process hit -- the forecast archetype nailed it. I flagged this as one of the round's CLOSEST ties and, crucially, an OPEN, end-to-end, higher-scoring game (BTTS 54%, Over 2.5 50%) -- NOT a low-block grind -- with a dangerous Senegal underdog and a real ET/penalty risk. Result: a 3-2 five-goal thriller (BTTS Yes ✅, way Over ✅), level after 90', that went to EXTRA TIME (the model's 26% ET branch) and was decided by the favourite with a 120+5 winner. Belgium advanced as the slight favourite (61.4%), exactly the split -- but Senegal (38.6%) was a kick from taking it further. The 1-1 mode was the single most likely scoreline, but the OPEN-game distribution I described (high BTTS/Over, wide scoreline spread) is what covered a 3-2. Both the side AND the game-shape (open, goals, deep) were right.
_postmatch.lesson: The open-vs-open archetype (two attacking sides, small class gap) correctly predicts a HIGH-scoring, close, deep-into-the-game tie -- the opposite texture of the tight low-block ties that grind to penalties. Weight the underdog's ATTACKING quality (Senegal), not just 'is it close': it produced an end-to-end 3-2. The favourite advanced but had to survive extra time against a genuinely dangerous side, precisely the 61/39 advancement split. Don't confuse 'close tie' with 'low-scoring tie' -- shape depends on both sides' attacking intent. AND, echoing England's HT comeback: Senegal actually led 2-0 before Belgium recovered to 2-2 by 90' and won in ET -- a quality favourite is not written off by an early/large deficit; the advancement number (61%) already priced Belgium's likelihood to get there even through adversity.
